Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:
General
âą References were updated throughout.
DCIS-1
âą Workup, last bullet added: Assess for distress (Also for IBC-1)
âą Footnote f added: Refer to the Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the Oncology Guidelines Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. (Also for 
BINV-1, PHYLL-1, PREG-1, PAGET-1, IBC-1)
âą Footnote h revised: Surgical axillary staging should not be performed for preoperative (biopsy-determined) pure DCIS unless there is some clinical-radiographic-pathologic suggestion of invasion 
or axillary metastasis. SLNB may be considered in 1) concern for persistently positive margins 2) excision in an anatomic location compromising the performance of a future SLNB procedure; or 
3) oncoplastic breast-conserving surgery (see BINV-D for considerations for SLNB).
âą Footnote i revised: An SLNB may be considered in the setting of 1) mastectomy for DCIS., 2) excision in an anatomic location compromising the performance of a future SLNB procedure 3) 
oncoplastic breast conserving surgery (see BINV-D for appropriate candidates for SLNB). Omission of SLNB in the setting of mastectomy may be considered in patients >50 years with non-
palpable low-grade ER-positive DCIS with no abnormal axillary findings and in patients in whom the primary reason for mastectomy is patient preference.
âą Footnote n revised: Whole breast RT following BCS reduces ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (IBTR) rates in for all DCIS by 50%â70%. Approximately half of the recurrences are invasive and 
half are DCIS. The risk of local relapse increases with larger DCIS, palpable mass, grade III disease, margins â€2 mm, ER-negative tumors, and age <50. Select patients with "low" risk DCIS may 
be considered suitable for omission of RT (endocrine therapy alone, if all RTOG 9804 criteria are met: screen-detected, grade 1â2, size â€2.5 cm, margins â„3 mm). Omission of RT and treatment 
with endocrine therapy alone may be considered if all RTOG 9804 criteria are met (screen-detected, grade 1â2, size â€2.5 cm, margins â„3 mm).
DCIS-2
âą DCIS Postsurgical Treatment, header revised: Risk reduction therapy for ipsilateral breast following BCS
BINV-1
âą Workup
Bullet 2, sub-bullet 2 revised: Breast ultrasound as necessary
Bullet 2, sub-sub-bullet added: Axillary ultrasound if considering omission of surgical nodal staging
âą cT1-4 pathway, last column, bullet 1 revised: BCS Â± surgical axillary staging + Â± whole breast RT
âą Footnote b added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-negative, grade 1â2 
tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with lobular histology. (Also for 
BINV-2)
BINV-2
âą Header revised: Locoregional Treatment of cT1â3, cN0 or CN+, M0 Disease: Breast-Conserving Surgery (BCS) + Â± Whole Breast RT
âą Column 3 header revised: Pathologic Nodal Status
âą Column 4, last pathway, option revised: Consider APBI/PBI in for appropriately selected patients who are low risk (category 1)
âą Footnote r added: Based on data from the Europa trial (Meattini I, et al. Lancet 2025;26;37-50), older patients eligible for omission of RT may choose to receive RT alone instead of endocrine 
therapy with no adverse effects on early breast cancer outcomes.
âą Footnote removed: Sentinel node biopsy may be omitted based on the SSO Choosing Wisely recommendation in patients â„70 years of age with HR+/HER2-negative and pT1, cN0 tumors 
(Hughes KS, et al. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:2382-2387. Kunkler IH, et al. N Engl J Med 2023:388:585-594).
BINV-3
âą Column 3
Top pathway revised: Negative axillary nodes (pN0/pN0[i+]) and â€pT2 (â€5 cm) and negative margins â„1 mm
Bottom pathway revised: Any pT/pN with positive margins Margins positive
Pathway removed: Negative axillary nodes and â€pT2 (â€5 cm) and negative margins but <1 mm.
âą Footnote x revised: In the case of pN1mi (a micrometastasis [>0.2 to â€2.0 mm]), and no axillary dissection, evaluate other patient risk factors when considering RT. 
âą Footnote y revised: PMRT may be considered for patients with multiple high-risk recurrence factors, including central/medial tumors or tumors â„2 cm and at least one of the following: 
grade 3, ER-negative, young age/longevity, or LVI.
âą Footnote z added: In the case of pT3N0, high-risk features for considering PMRT include, but are not limited to, young age/longevity and/or LVI.
BINV-5
âą Footnote mm revised: Updated results from the adjuvant APHINITY trial in HER2-positive early breast cancer, with a median follow-up of 8.4 11.3 years, have confirmed the benefit of 
adding Pertuzumab to Trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in preventing recurrences. (Also for subsequent pages)
Updates in Version 2.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:
BINV-Q (3 of 15)
âą First line
Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy + Pembrolizumab changed to a category 1 recommendation. 
Datopotamab deruxtecan-dlnk changed to a preferred recommendation.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
BINV-12
âą Additional Workup
Bullet 1, sub-bullet revised: Consider Axillary ultrasound unless prior MRI demonstrated negative axillary nodes
âą Footnote tt revised: If considering preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for postmenopausal patients with cN0, operable ER-
positive, HER2-negative disease (Iwata H, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2019;173:123-133; Pease AM, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2019;26:366-371). If considering 
preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for pre- and postmenopausal patients with cN0 and postmenopausal patients with cN1, 
operable ER-positive, HER2-negative disease.
BINV-14
âą Column 3
Top pathway revised: Adjuvant systemic therapy (complete planned regimen course if not completed preoperatively) + whole breast RT
 âBullet 2 revised: cN1 and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT Â± boost to the tumor bed.; and strongly consider Comprehensive RNI is not routinely indicated with inclusion
of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.
 âBullet 3 added: cN2â3 and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT Â± boost to the tumor bed with comprehensive RNI including any portion of the axilla at risk.
 âBullet 4 revised: Any ypN+: Adjuvant whole breast RT Â± boost to the tumor bed ; and with comprehensive RNI with inclusion of including any portion of the undissected 
axilla at risk.
Bottom pathway revised: Adjuvant systemic therapy (complete planned regimen course if not completed preoperatively) + Â± PMRT + RNI (after axilla adequately assessed
by SLNB, TAD, or ALND)
 âBullet 1 added: cT1âT3, cN0, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated.
 âBullet 2 added: cT1âT3, cN1, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated.
 âBullet 3 added: cN2â3 and ypN0; any ypN+; cT4 any N: PMRT + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the axilla at risk.
 âBottom pathway removed: Adjuvant systemic therapy, no PMRT
 âSub-bullets removed:
â cN+ and ypN0: Strongly consider PMRT to the chest wall and comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.
â Any ypN+: PMRT is indicated to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.
â cT4, any N: RT is indicated to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.
â cT3, cN0, and ypN0: Consider PMRT to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.
âą Footnote xx revised: Complete planned systemic therapy regimen course, if not completed preoperatively. Among patients shown to be N+ prior to preoperative systemic 
therapy, SLNB has a >10% false-negative rate when performed after preoperative systemic therapy, which can be improved by marking the most suspicious biopsied node 
prior to treatment, and at the time of surgery, using dual tracers, obtaining â„3 sentinel lymph nodes nodes and removal of the marked node (Targeted Axillary Dissection 
[TAD]).
âą Footnote yy revised: Strongly consider RT boost for high-risk features (eg, high-grade disease, age <50 years central/medial tumors, tumors â„2 cm, with â„1 of the following: 
grade 3, ER-negative, LVI, high residual cancer in the breast, or young age/longevity). 
âą Footnote zz revised: When considering PMRT + RNI for cN1 that has become ypN0 (after preoperative therapy), based on limited followup duration of NSABP B-51 study 
(Mamounas EP, et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2113-2124) and the inability to demonstrate long-term non-inferiority for this subgroup, a discussion of  potential risks of under-
treatment should be weighed against the previously established long-term survival benefits of PMRT+RNI, particularly for patients of younger age/longevity with ER-positive 
tumors, residual disease in the breast, and/or those with multiple traditional clinical pathologic high-risk features. (Mamounas E, Bandos H, White J, et al. Loco-regional 
irradiation in patients with biopsy-proven axillary node Involvement at presentation who become pathologically node-negative after neoadjuvant chemotherapy: Primary 
outcomes of NRG Oncology/NSABP B-51/RTOG 1304; Abstract GS02-07; SABCS 2023.)
BINV-16
âą Top pathway, column 2 revised: Adjuvant endocrine therapy (category 1), consider ribociclib for eligible patients, see BINV-K 2 of 3 for eligibility criteria.
âą ypT1-4,N0 or ypNâ„1 pathway revised to include: "or fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (category 1) for those with high risk of recurrence (see BINV-M 4 of 10 for high-risk 
criteria)"
BINV-17
âą Breast imaging
Bullet 2 revised: Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy
âą Lifestyle
Bullet 2 added: Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with caution in those with 
increased genetic cancer risk. See Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.
Footnote ddd revised: Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and 
RT with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance. 
Suspicious findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging should warrant immediate follow-up/workup might warrant a shorter interval between mammograms.

Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
BINV-18
âą Biomarker testing, sub-bullet 3 revised: Comprehensive germline and somatic profiling Multigene panel testing (MGPT) (somatic and germline) to identify candidates for 
targeted therapies
âą Footnote kkk revised: In the setting of oligometastatic breast cancer, the currently available data do not support ablative metastasis-directed RT (ie, stereotactic body 
radiation therapy [SBRT]) for extending overall survival (OS) or progression-free survival (PFS). In some cases, SBRT may be preferred over palliative RT to provide 
more durable local control and pain relief. Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional 
treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may 
reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms. Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better 
outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term disease-
free survival (DFS) for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to âno evidence of diseaseâ (NED). For de novo 
oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, 
bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all 
sites of metastases, and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be considered. (Also for BINV-21)
BINV-19
âą Column 3
3rd row revised: Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated then surgical resection if feasible + consider surgical axillary staging + PMRT
4th row revised: Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated then surgical resection if feasible + consider surgical axillary staging if + repeat 
RT, if feasible and indicated
âą Column 4 revised: Consider appropriate adjuvant systemic therapy as clinically indicated and Supportive Care and see Principles of Survivorship (Also for BINV-20)
âą Footnote removed: Consider systemic therapy to best response, then resect if possible. (Also for subsequent pages)
âą Footnote removed: See the Discussion for additional information. (Also for subsequent pages)
BINV-20
âą 1st row revised: Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated, then surgical resection if feasible with ALND (for axillary recurrence) + RT if 
feasible
âą 2nd row revised: Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated, then surgical resection of gross disease Â± ALND (for axillary recurrence) if 
feasible + RT if feasible
BINV-21
âą Footnote removed: Routine surgical resection of the primary breast tumor is generally not indicated in the management of patients presenting with de novo stage IV 
(M1) disease. Although there is no survival benefit, it may be considered for local control of the primary tumor. Discussion regarding management of the primary tumor 
in this setting must be individualized. 
BINV-22
âą Section significantly revised. 
BINV-23
âą Terminology was changed from systemic therapy to cytotoxic therapy. (Also for subsequent pages)
âą Column 2, revised: If not endocrine therapy refractory, consider: Alternate endocrine therapy Â± targeted therapy (if premenopausal, consider ovarian
âą ablation or suppression).
âą Footnote ttt added: Cytotoxic therapy includes chemotherapy or antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs), or targeted therapy. (Also for subsequent pages)
BINV-A
âą Section significantly revised. 
BINV-B
âą Clinical Indications and Applications
Bullet 1 revised: May be used for staging evaluation to define extent of cancer or presence of multifocal or multicentric cancer in the ipsilateral breast, or as screening 
of the contralateral breast cancer at time of initial diagnosis (category 2B). There are no high-level data to demonstrate that the use of MRI to facilitate local therapy 
decision-making improves local recurrence or survival Available data does not demonstrate that the use of preoperative MRI improves re-excision rates, local 
recurrence, or survival for DCIS or invasive breast cancer in patients pursuing BCS.

âą Right column
Bullet 2 revised: Alternative methods of birth control include non-hormonalâbased intrauterine devices (IUDs), barrier methods, or, for patients with no intent of future 
pregnancies, tubal ligation or vasectomy for the partner
Last bullet revised: Breastfeeding following after breast-conservation therapy cancer treatment is not contraindicated...
BINV-D
âą Column 1, top pathway added: No palpable lymph node at diagnosis and negative axillary ultrasound
âą SLN positive pathway
Bullet 1 revised: cT1âT2, T3h cN0 or pN1mic
Bullet 3 revised: pN1mic; 1â2 positive SLNs
âą Footnote e added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-
negative, grade 1â2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used

BINV-E
âą Footnote added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-
negative, grade 1â2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used 
in those with lobular histology.
âą Footnote removed: Sentinel node biopsy may be omitted based on the SSO Choosing Wisely recommendation in patients â„70 years of age with HR+/HER2-negative 
and pT1, cN0 tumors (Hughes KS, et al. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:2382-2387. Kunkler IH, et al. N Engl J Med 2023:388:585-594). 
âą Footnote b removed: Based on emerging data from the SOUND trial, patients with HR+/HER2-negative tumors, pT1, N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), may be 
considered for omission of SLN biopsy. Gentilini OD, et al. JAMA Oncol 2023;9:1557-1564.
BINV-G
âą "For BCS" added to Absolute and Relative contraindications 
âą Absolute contraindications, bullet 5 revised: Patients diagnosed with gestational breast cancer (not receiving systemic chemotherapy) who cannot receive RT within 
12â16 weeks; see PREG-1
âą Absolute contraindications, bullet 6 revised: Multicentric disease with any of the following criteria (mandate mastectomy)
BINV-H (5 of 7)
âą Bullet 7revised: Skin- and nipple-sparing mastectomy should be performed by an experienced breast surgery team that works in a coordinated, multidisciplinary 
fashion to guide proper patient selection for skin-sparing mastectomy, determine optimal sequencing of the reconstructive procedure(s) in relation to adjuvant 
therapies, and perform a resection that achieves appropriate surgical margins.
âą Last bullet added: Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy but diagnostic imaging may be required for new symptomatic concerns.
BINV-I (1 of 3) 
âą Optimizing Delivery of Individual Therapy,
Bullet 1, sub-bullet 5 last sentence added: Consider daily imaging for patients receiving prone breast radiotherapy.
âą Whole Breast RT, bullet 2, sub-bullet 3 revised: Available data support the use of ultra-hypofractionated regimens whole breast RT of (28.5 Gy in 5 (once-a-
week fractions) or 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions). The optimal way to incorporate a tumor bed boost and the long-term toxicity associated with its delivery in the 
ultrahypofractionated setting is yet to be established. fractions may be considered for selected patients over 50 years following BCS with early-stage, node-negative 
disease, particularly those in whom a boost is not intended. 
âą Footnote c added: Daily image guidance in conjunction with regular 3D assessments (eg, cone beam CT, SGRT) for IMRT/VMAT techniques.
âą Footnote removed: Alternatively, 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions over one week may be considered, though data beyond 5 years for local relapse or toxicity are not yet 
available for this regimen. (Murray Brunt A, Haviland JS, Wheatley DA, et al. Hypofractionated breast radiotherapy for 1 week versus 3 weeks [FAST-Forward]: 5-year 
efficacy and late normal tissue effects results from a multicentre, non-inferiority, randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2020;395:1613-1626.)
âą Footnote removed: Brunt AM, Haviland JS, Sydenham M, et al. Ten-year results of FAST: A randomized controlled trial of 5-fraction whole-breast radiotherapy for early 
breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:3261-3272.

BINV-I (2 of 3)
âą PMRT (including breast reconstruction)  
Last bullet added: For patients with positive surgical margins after neoadjuvant systemic therapy, re-excision to negative margins is preferred. If not feasible, then 
strongly consider PMRT to chest wall Â± comprehensive RNI (including any portion of the undissected axilla at risk).
Bullet removed: In the case of cT3N0, high-risk features for considering PMRT include, but are not limited to, young age and/or LVI. 
âą RNI 
Bullet 3 added: RNI generally includes supraclavicular, infraclavicular, internal mammary and axillary nodes at risk. Coverage of the internal mammary nodes may be 
individually determined based on tumor location (medial/central), tumor size, extent of nodal involvement, and dose to the OAR.
Bullet 4, sub-bullet 1 revised: RT doses to the regional nodes of 46â50 Gy (conventional fractionation) or 39â42.56 Gy (moderately fractionated) dosing schedules 
are similar to PMRT and whole breast may be considered.
Footnote d added: Bolus should be used for patients with skin involvement, positive superficial margins, and those with dermal lymphatic invasion and/or extensive 
LVI.
Footnote e added: Preliminary data show similar normal tissue toxicity at 5 years with 26 Gy in 5 fractions, for patients receiving regional nodal irradiation without 
inclusion of internal mammary nodes. Longer-term toxicity and efficacy data are not yet available, and limited data are available in the context of reconstruction. (UK 
FF Nodal Sub-study; Murray Brunt et al, Rad Onc 2025).
BINV-I (3 of 3)
âą Bullet 1 revised: The Oncology Guidelines Panel endorses APBI/PBI for any patient without germline BRCA1/2 PVs who meets the criteria outlined in the 2023 2024 ASTRO 
guidelines. Patients aged â„40 years are recommended for APBI/PBI if they have: 
BINV-K (1 of 4) 
âą General Principles
Bullet 1, sub-bullet 1 revised: ER+ tumors: ER testing should be used to determine if a patient is a candidate for endocrine therapies. Patients with cancers with 
1%â100% ER IHC staining are considered ER+ and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are limited efficacy data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-low-
positive (1%â10%) results. The ER-low-positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus, individualized 
consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-making. High ER expression is 
more likely to benefit from endocrine therapy; however, the potential benefit of endocrine therapy should be discussed even with low ER expression.
Bullet 3, sub-bullet 1 revised: Level of ER expression: Low ER+ expression is less likely to benefit from endocrine therapy. High ER+ expression is more likely to 
benefit from endocrine therapy; however, the potential benefit of endocrine therapy should be discussed even with low ER expression.
Footnotes from subsequent pages were added as bullets 4, 5, and 6.
BINV-K (2 of 4)
âą Ovarian function assessment
Bullet 2 revised: Monitor estradiol and Â± follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)/luteinizing hormone (LH) levels:
Bullet 2, last sub-bullet revised: Prior to next dose of GNRH agonist, particularly in women under 45 years. Frequency of testing of estradiol and Â± FSH/LH levels 
should be individualized. 
Last bullet added: If patient is not postmenopausal, sequential evaluation of hormonal status is recommended.
âą Methods for OFS, bullet 1, sub-bullet 3 revised: Triptorelin: 3.75 mg IM every 4 weeks or 11.25 mg IM every 12 weeks.
âą Header revised: Options for Initiation of OFS
Bullet 1 revised: Prior to or with start of chemotherapy (neoadjuvant preoperative or adjuvant)
âą Duration of OFS
Bullet 1 revised: 5 years of OFS is optimal based on SOFT and TEXT trials. The 8-year DFS was 85.4% with OFS + Tamoxifen versus 80.2% with Tamoxifen alone. 
The 15-year DFS in SOFT trial was 73.5% with OFS + exemestane versus 70.5% with OFS + tamoxifen versus 67.0% for tamoxifen alone. A minimum 2 years of 
OFS is encouraged. There are no data on efficacy or safety to support prolonging of OFS >5 years.

BINV-Q (1 of 15)
âą Header revised: Systemic Cytotoxic Therapy for Recurrent Unresectable (Local or Regional) or Stage IV (M1) Disease
âą General considerations, bullets added:
The selection, dosing, and administration of anti-cancer agents and the management of associated toxicities are complex. Modifications of drug dose and schedule 
and initiation of supportive care interventions are often necessary because of expected toxicities and individual patient variability, prior treatment, and comorbidity. 
The optimal delivery of anti-cancer agents therefore requires a health care delivery team experienced in the use of anti-cancer agents and the management of 
associated toxicities in patients with cancer.
For information regarding DPYD testing, see the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer. 
Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-
pmph has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab.
Bullet 6, sub-bullet 2 revised: Consider cryotherapy of hands and feet to decrease the risk of peripheral neuropathy. Use of glove constriction may be considered as it 
helps with reducing peripheral neuropathy.
âą For Those Receiving HER2-Targeted Therapy
Bullet 2 revised: Pertuzumab, Trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV Pertuzumab and Trastuzumab; however, the
dosing and administration instruction may differ. Pertuzumab, trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf injection for subcutaneous use may be substituted anywhere that 
the combination of intravenous pertuzumab and intravenous trastuzumab are given as part of systemic therapy. Pertuzumab, trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf 
injection for subcutaneous use has different dosing and administration instructions compared to the intravenous products.

âą First line, row 2: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy was added as a category 1, preferred recommendation. 
âą First line, row 2: Datopotamab deruxtecan-dlnk was added as a category 2A, other recommended recommendation.

âą First line row 3: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki + Pertuzumab was added as a category 2A, other recommended recommendation. 
âą Second/Third Line row combined.

with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing safety or toxicity of this drug in a trial.
âą Footnote q revised: Single-agent fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki may be considered in the first-line setting as an option for select patients (ie, those with rapid 
progression within 6 months of neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy [12 months for Pertuzumab-containing regimens]). Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is associated 
with ILD/pneumonitis. Regular monitoring for this serious side effect is recommended. For patients with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing

reflex testing with tissue-based NGS assay.
âą Footnote w added: Tissue biopsy is preferred to ctDNA at detecting homozygous copy loss of PTEN or TMB. 
âą Footnote removed: The safety of Alpelisib in patients with Type 1 or uncontrolled Type 2 diabetes has not been established.
BINV-Q (7 of 15)
âą Footnote removed: Tissue biopsy is more sensitive than ctDNA (liquid biopsy) at detecting homozygous copy loss of PTEN or TMB-H. (Also for BINV-Q 7)

âą Footnote a revised: CT scans and nuclear imaging (bone scan or PET/CT) and contrast-enhanced MRI are contraindicated during pregnancy. 
âą Footnote removed: Considerations and selection of optimal local therapy and systemic therapy are similar to that recommended in nonâpregnancy-associated 
breast cancer; see other sections of this guideline. However, the selection and timing of chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, and RT is different if pregnant. BCS 
during the first trimester of pregnancy can be considered in those who will require adjuvant chemotherapy and can have adjuvant RT delayed until after delivery. 
Chemotherapy should not be administered during the first trimester of pregnancy, and RT should not be administered during any trimester of pregnancy. Coordination 
is recommended between the oncology and obstetrics teams to plan the optimal timing of systemic therapy administration during pregnancy. Most experience 
with chemotherapy during pregnancy for breast cancer is from regimens that utilize various combinations of Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, and Fluorouracil. 
Considerations for postpartum chemotherapy are the same as for nonâpregnancy-associated breast cancer. 
âą Footnote removed: Use of blue dye is contraindicated in pregnancy; radiolabeled sulfur colloid appears to be safe for SLNB in pregnancy. See Considerations for 
Surgical Axillary Staging (BINV-D).

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
DCIS-1
a Encapsulated papillary carcinoma (EPC) without associated conventional 
invasion is staged as pTis because behavior is similar to DCIS (per AJCC). SPC 
should be specified as in situ or invasive based on WHO criteria but both forms 
have favorable outcomes. 
b The Panel endorses the College of American Pathologists Protocol for 
pathology reporting for all invasive and noninvasive carcinomas of the breast. 
http://www.cap.org.
c For risk criteria, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk 
Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate.
d Principles of Dedicated Breast MRI Testing (BINV-B).
e The use of MRI has not been shown to increase likelihood of negative margins 
or decrease conversion to mastectomy. Data to support improved long-term 
outcomes are lacking.
f Refer to the Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the Oncology Guidelines 
Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of 
health.
g Re-resection(s) may be performed in an effort to obtain negative margins in 
patients desiring breast-conservation therapy. Patients in whom adequate 
surgical margins cannot be achieved with BCS should undergo a total 
mastectomy. For definition of adequate surgical margins, see Margin Status 
Recommendations After BCS for Invasive Cancers and DCIS (BINV-F).

and age <50. Omission of RT and treatment with endocrine therapy alone may be

Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS)
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
DCIS-2
o CYP2D6 genotype testing is not recommended for patients considering tamoxifen.
p Available data suggest endocrine therapy provides risk reduction in the ipsilateral breast treated with breast conservation and in the contralateral breast in patients 
with mastectomy or breast conservation with ER-positive primary tumors. Since a survival advantage has not been demonstrated, individual consideration of risks and 
benefits is important.
q The use of a bisphosphonate (PO/IV) or denosumab is acceptable to maintain or improve bone mineral density (BMD) and reduce risk of fractures in postmenopausal 
(natural or induced) patients receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy. Optimal duration of either therapy has not been established. Benefits from duration beyond 
3 years or optimal duration beyond 3 years is not known. Factors to consider for duration of anti-osteoporosis therapy include BMD, response to therapy, and risk 
factors for continued bone loss or fracture. There are case reports of spontaneous fractures after denosumab discontinuation. Patients treated with a bisphosphonate 
or denosumab should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to the initiation of therapy, and should take supplemental calcium and vitamin D. 
r Low-dose tamoxifen (5 mg per day or 10 mg every other day for 3â5 years) is an option if patient is symptomatic on the 20-mg dose or if patient is unwilling or 
unable to take standard-dose 20 mg per day tamoxifen [10 mg every other day since 5-mg dose is not available in the United States]. This low dosage needs further 
investigation in premenopausal individuals (DeCensi A, et al. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1629-1637).
Risk reduction therapy for ipsilateral breast:
âą Consider endocrine therapy for 5 years for patients with ER-
positive DCIS, if:
Treated with BCS and RTo (category 1) 
Treated with excision alonep
âą Endocrine therapyq:
Tamoxifeno,r for premenopausal patients
Tamoxifeno,r or aromatase inhibitor for postmenopausal 
patients with some advantage for aromatase inhibitor therapy 
in patients <60 years or with concerns for thromboembolism
Risk reduction therapy for contralateral breast:
âą Counseling regarding risk reduction

SURVEILLANCE/FOLLOW-UP
âą Interval history and physical exam every 6â12 mo for 5 y, 
then annually
âą First mammogram 6â12 mo, after end of RT, if given, or 
after BCS (category 2B) if no RT is given and annually 
thereafter

aa According to WHO, carcinoma of no special type (NST) encompasses 
multiple patterns including medullary pattern, cancers with neuroendocrine 
expression, and other rare patterns.
bb There are rare subtypes of metaplastic carcinoma (low-grade 
adenosquamous and low-grade fibromatosis-like carcinoma) that are 
considered to have a favorable prognosis without adjuvant systemic therapies. 
cc To be associated with favorable prognosis, the favorable histologic type 
should not be high grade, should be pure (>90% as classified on the surgical 
excision, not core biopsy alone), and should be HER2 negative. If atypical 
pathologic or clinical features are present, consider treating as ductal/NST.

jj The absolute benefit of HER2-targeted systemic therapy is negligible in patients 
with HR-positive cancers and tumor size bordering on T1mic (<1 mm), and 
endocrine therapy remains a viable option for systemic treatment. 
kk Consider adjuvant bisphosphonate therapy for risk reduction of distant 
metastasis for 3â5 years in postmenopausal patients (natural or induced) with 
high-risk node-negative or node-positive tumors.
ll Consider extended adjuvant neratinib following adjuvant trastuzumab-containing 
therapy for patients with HR-positive, HER2-positive disease with a perceived 
high risk of recurrence. The benefit or toxicities associated with extended 
neratinib in patients who have received pertuzumab or ado-trastuzumab 
emtansine is unknown.
mm Updated results from the adjuvant APHINITY trial in HER2-positive early 
breast cancer, with a median follow-up of 11.3 years, have confirmed the 
benefit of adding pertuzumab to trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in preventing 
recurrences.

subgroup of cancers with ER-lowâpositive (1%â10%) results. The ER-lowâpositive 
group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative 
cancers; thus individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine 
therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-
making. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A).

a	For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see Oncology Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. 
b Breast MRI may be useful for characterizing axillary and/or internal mammary nodal disease. See Principles of Dedicated Breast MRI Testing (BINV-B) and Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis.
tt If considering preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for pre- and postmenopausal patients with cN0 and postmenopausal 
patients with cN1, operable ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. (Iwata H, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2019;173:123â133; Ma SJ, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 
2021;189:737â745; Hanson K, et al. J Clin Oncol 2021;39.15_suppl.591; Beitsch PD, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;41(16_suppl):Abstract 523).
uu At the time of biopsy of the most suspicious axillary lymph node(s), a marker should be placed to allow for identification and removal at the time of definitive surgery.
vv FDG-PET/CT is most beneficial and accurate for advanced disease (stage III) and invasive ductal (compared to lobular) histology, but may be useful in selected 
circumstances of earlier stage disease (stage IIA disease: cT1cN1, cT2cN0) such as: equivocal CT+ bone scan results; suspicion of undetected nodal and/or 
distant disease; and treatment response assessment. An FDG-PET/CT may be utilized as an adjunct to, or in lieu of, initial standard staging and may be performed 
simultaneously with diagnostic CT. Conversely, a bone scan or sodium fluoride PET/CT may not be needed if an upfront FDG-PET/CT clearly indicates consistent 
findings on both PET and CT components.

k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J).
p Includes techniques such as local tissue rearrangement, local flaps, regional flaps, breast reduction, and mastopexy to allow for greater volumes of resection while 
optimizing aesthetic outcomes in patients undergoing BCS.
t Principles of Radiation Therapy (BINV-I).
w Principles of Breast Reconstruction Following Surgery (BINV-H).
ww The accurate assessment of in-breast tumor or regional lymph node response to preoperative systemic therapy is difficult, and should include physical examination 
and performance of imaging studies (mammogram and/or breast ultrasound and/or breast MRI) that were abnormal at the time of initial tumor staging. Selection of 
imaging methods prior to surgery should be determined by the multidisciplinary team. MRI is more accurate than mammography for assessing tumor response to 
preoperative therapy.
xx Among patients shown to be N+ prior to preoperative systemic therapy, SLNB has a >10% false-negative rate when performed after preoperative systemic therapy, 
which can be improved by marking the most suspicious biopsied node prior to treatment, and at the time of surgery, using dual tracers, obtaining â„3 sentinel lymph 
nodes nodes and removal of the marked node (Targeted Axillary Dissection [TAD]).
yy Strongly consider RT boost for high-risk features (eg, central/medial tumors, tumors â„2 cm with â„1 of the following: grade 3, ER-negative, LVI, high residual cancer in 
the breast, or young age/longevity). 
zz When considering PMRT + RNI for cN1 that has become ypN0 (after preoperative therapy), based on limited followup duration of NSABP B-51 study (Mamounas EP, 
et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2113-2124) and the inability to demonstrate long-term non-inferiority for this subgroup, a discussion of  potential risks of under-treatment 
should be weighed against the previously established long-term survival benefits of PMRT+RNI, particularly for patients of younger age/longevity with ER-positive 
tumors, residual disease in the breast, and/or those with multiple traditional clinical pathologic high-risk features.

with capecitabine or olaparib in patients who meet criteria for treatment with 
one or more of these agents. However, their sequential/combined use may be 
considered given high risk of recurrence in patients with residual disease.
For high risk: Adjuvant pembrolizumab (if pembrolizumab-containing regimen was given 
preoperatively)
Adjuvant endocrine therapy (category 1)
+ adjuvant olaparib if germline BRCA1/2 PV (category 1). Consider adjuvant abemaciclib or ribociclib 
for eligible patients, see BINV-K 2 of 4 for eligibility criteria.

BINV-17
SURVEILLANCE/FOLLOW-UP
Recurrent 
Disease 
(BINV-18)
ddd Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and RT with no clear 
advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance. Suspicious 
findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging should warrant immediate follow-up/workup.
eee The use of estrogen, progesterone, or selective ER modulators to treat osteoporosis or osteopenia in patients with breast cancer is discouraged. The use of a 
bisphosphonate (oral/IV) or denosumab is acceptable to maintain or to improve BMD and reduce risk of fractures in postmenopausal (natural or induced) patients 
receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy. Optimal duration of either therapy has not been established. Benefits of duration beyond 3 years or optimal duration 
beyond 3 years is not known. Factors to consider for duration of anti-osteoporosis therapy include BMD, response to therapy, and risk factors for continued bone loss 
or fracture. There are case reports of spontaneous fractures after denosumab discontinuation. Patients treated with a bisphosphonate or denosumab should undergo 
a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to the initiation of therapy, and should take supplemental calcium and vitamin D.
Exam:
âą History and physical exam 1â4 times per year as clinically 
appropriate for 5 years, then annually
Genetic screening:
âą Periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic 
testing indications and referral to genetic counseling as 
indicated, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-
Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
Postsurgical management:
âą Educate, monitor, and refer for lymphedema management, 
see Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship: Lymphedema
Breast imaging:
âą Mammography every 12 months, beginning 6 months or 
more after completion of breast-conservation therapyddd
âą Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated 
after mastectomy 
âą For patients with germline mutations or family history of 
breast cancer, please refer to Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/
Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, 
and Prostate
Fertility, birth control, and sexual health (see BINV-C)
Screening for metastases:
âą In the absence of clinical signs and symptoms suggestive 
of recurrent disease, there is no indication for laboratory or 
imaging studies for metastases screening.  
Post-treatment monitoring: 
âą Cardiotoxicity monitoring for patients who received left-
sided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy. For 
anthracycline-induced toxicity, see Oncology Guidelines for 
Survivorship
âą Provide guidance on risk of comorbidities
Endocrine therapy:
âą Assess and encourage adherence to adjuvant endocrine 
therapy
âą Patients on tamoxifen: 
Age-appropriate gynecologic screening
Routine annual pelvis ultrasound is not recommended
âą Patients on an aromatase inhibitor or who experience ovarian 
failure secondary to treatment should have monitoring of bone 
health with a bone mineral density (BMD) determination at 
baseline and periodically thereaftereee
Lifestyle:
âą Evidence suggests that active lifestyle, healthy diet, limited 
alcohol intake, and achieving and maintaining an ideal body 
weight (20â25 body mass index [BMI]) may lead to optimal breast 
cancer outcomes. See Oncology Guidelines for Breast Cancer Risk 
Reduction
âą Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in 
survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with 
caution in those with increased genetic cancer risk. See Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Survivorship
Psychosocial support:
âą Survivors are at elevated risk for fear of recurrence, distress, 
anxiety, and depression that may persist for many years after 
diagnosis. Periodic screening and referral to mental health 
professionals if needed are recommended. See Oncology Guidelines 
for Survivorship 
Communication:
âą Coordination of care between the primary care physician (PCP) 
and specialists is encouraged. Additionally, a personalized 
survivorship treatment plan including personalized treatment 
summary of possible long-term toxicity and clear follow-up 
recommendations is recommended. See Oncology Guidelines for 
Survivorship
Engagement:
âą Patients frequently require follow-up encouragement in order 
to improve adherence to ongoing screening and medication 
adherence. See Oncology Guidelines for Patients

e Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A).
f For risk criteria, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, 
Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate.
h Refer to the Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the Oncology Guidelines Distress 
Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health.
fff For the treatment of brain metastases, see Oncology Guidelines for Central Nervous System 
Cancers.
ggg False-negative ER and/or PR determinations occur, and there may be discordance 
between the ER and/or PR determination between the primary and metastatic tumor(s). 
Therefore, endocrine therapy with its low attendant toxicity may be considered in 
patients with non-visceral or asymptomatic visceral tumors, especially in patients 
with clinical characteristics predicting for an HR-positive tumor (eg, long disease-free 
interval, limited sites of recurrence, indolent disease, older age).
hhh In clinical situations where a biopsy cannot safely be obtained but the clinical evidence 
is strongly supportive of recurrence, treatment may commence based on the ER/PR/
HER2 status of the primary tumor. Since ER/PR and HER2 status can change with 
treatment and metastatic progression, it may be appropriate to consider repeat testing 
on new samples in these scenarios if management will change.
iii Tumor tissue or plasma-based circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) assays may be used and 
each of these have benefits and limitations for diagnosis and disease progression. Tissue-
based assays have greater sensitivity for some alterations, but ctDNA may reflect tumor 
heterogeneity more accurately. If one specimen is negative for actionable biomarkers, 
testing on the alternative specimen can be considered.
jjj See Oncology Guidelines for Palliative Care and Oncology Guidelines for Supportive Care.
kkk Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. 
Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for 
metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective 
trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms. Compared 
with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer 
have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed 
in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term 
disease-free survival (DFS) for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who 
received multimodality therapy leading to âno evidence of diseaseâ (NED). For de novo 
oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after 
receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only 
metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving 
locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases, 
and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be 
considered.

lll Multidisciplinary approach is especially important in the management of breast cancer recurrence to consider all potential treatment options for optimal outcomes.
mmm In patients with a local breast recurrence after BCS who had a prior SLNB, a repeat SLNB may be considered although the accuracy of repeat SLNB is unproven. 
After mastectomy, repeat SLNB may be considered although there are limited data in this setting. In patients who had a prior axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) 
or mastectomy, repeat SLNB with lymphoscintigraphy may be considered rather than ALND (Poodt IGM, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2019;26:2417-2427). 
nnn Repeat BCS Â± adjuvant APBI/PBI may be considered in selected patients who decline mastectomy and otherwise meet consensus criteria for radiotherapy omission 
or APBI/PBI. There are limited data for a repeat BCS in this setting.
ooo The decision to use RT to treat locoregional recurrence must factor in any prior radiation to the area and the risk of late normal tissue toxicity from the sum of the 
prior and planned radiation courses.

ee Although patients with cancers with 1%â100% ER IHC staining are considered ER-positive and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are more limited data on the 
subgroup of cancers with ER-lowâpositive (1%â10%) results. The ER-lowâpositive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative 
cancers; thus, individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-making. 
See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A).
kkk Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative 
treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and 
palliate symptoms. Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions 
should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term DFS for patients with de novo oligometastatic 
breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to NED. For de novo oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status 
after receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality 
approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved 
long-term PFS and may be considered.
ppp Denosumab, zoledronic acid, or pamidronate (all with calcium and vitamin D supplementation) should be given (category 1) in addition to systemic therapy or 
endocrine therapy if bone metastasis is present, expected survival is â„3 months, and renal function is adequate. Patients should undergo a dental examination with 
preventive dentistry prior to initiation of this therapy. The optimal schedule for zoledronic acid is every 12 weeks. 
qqq Baseline assessment of bone density is recommended for patients receiving an aromatase inhibitor who are at risk of osteoporosis (eg, age >65, family history, 
chronic steroids).

tumor(s). Therefore, endocrine therapy with its low attendant toxicity may be considered in patients with non-visceral or asymptomatic visceral tumors, especially in 
patients with clinical characteristics predicting for an HR-positive tumor (eg, long disease-free interval, limited sites of recurrence, indolent disease, older age).

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026
Invasive Breast Cancer
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
BINV-28
General Principles 
âą The management unresectable, stage IV (M1) or metastatic disease requires a 
multifaceted approach, encompassing treatment and a comprehensive survivorship 
plan. 
âą For principles of monitoring metastatic disease, see BINV-R.
âą Encourage patients to promptly report any new or worsening symptoms. A 
comprehensive evaluation is essential, as these symptoms could indicate disease 
progression, treatment effects, or other unrelated medical issues. 
âą Additional health monitoring, management, and immunizations should be performed 
as appropriate to the prognosis under the care of a PCP.
âą Patients are encouraged to maintain a therapeutic relationship with a PCP throughout 
their lifetime.
Treatment Management and Supportive Care
âą Regular follow-up discussions about goals of care and care coordination may be 
necessary to improve both survival and quality of life. Engaging patients in these 
discussions, including therapy cessation, ensures that care aligns with their values 
and preferences. 
âą Surveillance and follow-up plans should be individualized for all patients, particularly 
older patients, considering comorbidities, functional status, and life expectancy. 
Personalization of care is key to managing the complexities of older adults or disease 
in those with multiple medical comorbidities.
âą Patients should receive multidisciplinary and interprofessional care that seeks to 
prevent and/or manage morbidities associated with breast cancer or its treatment.
âą Patients often experience multiple transitions between different care settings and 
health care providers. Coordination between primary care providers and specialists is 
essential to ensure continuity of care. 
âą Addressing all treatment options at each progression, including the option of 
supportive care alone or hospice care, is appropriate in patients with stage IV 
disease.
âą Early involvement of a palliative care team should be considered to provide advance 
care planning, goals of care discussions, care transitions, psychosocial support, and 
management of cancer and therapy-related symptoms. 
âą Routine screening, evaluation, and assessment for supportive care interventions and 
referrals are necessary. Some of the symptoms are:
Hormone-related symptoms (Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship)
Cognitive dysfunction (Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship)
Peripheral neuropathy
Cancer-related pain (Oncology Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain)
Cancer-related fatigue (Oncology Guidelines for Cancer-Related Fatigue)
Cancer-related infections (Oncology Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of 
Cancer-Related Infections)
Chemotherapy-induced anemia, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia (Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors)
Immunotherapy-related toxicities (Oncology Guidelines for Management of Immune 
Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities)

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURVIVORSHIP FOR THOSE LIVING WITH ADVANCED OR METASTATIC BREAST CANCER
content:
Cardiac monitoring for patients treated with anthracyclines or HER2-
targeted therapy (Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship)
Nausea/vomiting, anorexia, cachexia, diarrhea, dyspnea, malignant 
wounds, sleep-wake disturbances (Oncology Guidelines for Antiemesis and 
other Oncology Guidelines for Supportive Care)
Lifestyle, Wellness, and Psychosocial Support: 
âą Patients should be encouraged to maintain a physically active lifestyle and a 
healthy diet whenever possible. See Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.
âą Consider appropriate cancer and preventive health screenings per patient 
age and sex assigned-at-birth. These decisions should be individualized 
according to the patients prognosis.
âą Screen for anxiety, fear of progression, depression, and post-traumatic 
stress disorder (PTSD) related to cancer and its treatment. See 
Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management and Oncology Guidelines for 
Survivorship.
âą Social determinants of health monitoring and referrals
Provide resources and assistance through financial navigators, social 
workers, or other specified personnel to address financial concerns, 
employment issues, and family support. Ensuring access to these services 
can alleviate significant stress for patients. 
 âSee Oncology Guidelines for Palliative Care: Social Support/Resource 
Management. 
 âFinancial Advocacy Services Guidelines (Association of Cancer Care 
Centers).
 âFinancial Toxicity and Cancer Treatment (National Cancer Institute).
See Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management.
âą Facilitate connections with consumer groups, support networks, and 
organizations that advocate for accessible and equitable care. Engagement 
with these groups can empower patients and provide additional support.
âą For any patients with sexual health concerns or for fertility and birth control 
in premenopausal patients, see BINV-C.
Communication
âą Consistent and clear communication while acknowledging uncertainties of 
disease trajectory and prognosis is crucial. 
âą Treatment goals, survivorship issues, and the specific roles of each provider 
should be communicated to the patient.
Engagement
âą See Oncology Guidelines Resources for Patients and Caregivers and Guidelines for 
Patients. 
âą Address long-term survivorship issues specific to younger patients, such as 
psychosocial development and educational or vocational support. These 
patients may face unique challenges that require targeted interventions.  
âą Living will/end-of-life planning should be discussed.

header:
PRINCIPLES OF BIOMARKER TESTING
HER2 (ERBB2) TESTINGa
content:
âą HER2 testing should be performed on all new primary or newly metastatic breast cancers using methodology outlined in the ASCO/CAP HER2 testing 
guideline.a
âą A re-review of the pathology with consideration for repeat or consultative HER2 testing should be made if a grade 1 (any histologic type), pure mucinous, 
pure tubular, or pure cribriform carcinoma tests HER2-positive.a 
âą After a negative HER2 test result on initial biopsy sample, consider retesting on subsequent surgical or other additional sample if the initial sample was 
suboptimal (eg, minimal invasive cancer was present, cold ischemic time or fixation was suboptimal), testing error is expected, additional samples contain 
higher grade morphologically distinct cancer from the biopsy, to rule out heterogeneity in a high-grade cancer, or if it will otherwise aid in clinical decision-
making.a 
âą HER2 immunohistochemistry (IHC) results with a score of 0 should include the specific staining pattern present (0/absent membrane staining or 0+/with 
membrane staining) per current CAP Breast Cancer Biomarker Reporting Protocol.
âą Pathologists should be encouraged to examine any IHC 0/no staining carefully for any staining at high power, sinceÂ the distinction between HER2 
(ERBB2) IHC 0/absent membrane staining, IHC 0+/with membrane staining (faint, partial membrane staining in â€10%), IHC 1+, or 2+/ISH negative 
results (on primary or metastatic samples) is currently clinically relevant since patients with metastatic disease may be eligible for treatment targeting 
non-amplified levels of HER2 expression.  
âą Laboratory must participate in a quality assurance accreditation program for HER2 testing. Otherwise, tissue specimen should be sent to an accredited 
laboratory for testing. Health care systems and providers must cooperate to ensure the highest quality testing.
a	 Oncology Guidelines endorses the ASCO/CAP HER2 testing guideline. âPrinciples of HER2 Testingâ modified with permission from Wolff AC, Somerfield MR, Dowsett M, et al. Human 
epidermal growth factor receptor 2 testing in breast cancer. Arch Pathol Lab Med 2023;147:993-1000.
BINV-A

f	Single-probe ISH assays are not preferentially recommended but, if used, cases 
with averageÂ HER2Â (ERBB2) copy number â„4.0 and <6.0 signals/cell should 
base final results on concurrent IHC and if 2+ reflexed to dual probe ISH testing.
g	Evidence from trastuzumab adjuvant trials show that HER2 (ERBB2) testing 
by ISH or IHC have similar utility to predict clinical benefit from HER2-targeted 
therapy.

with recounting of the ISH test by a second reviewer if IHC is 2+ (per 2018 
CAP/ASCO Update recommendations). Additional report comments are 
recommended for negative final results in these ISH groups.

Discussion
âą HR testing (ER and PR) by IHC should be performed on any new primary or newly 
metastatic breast cancer using methodology outlined in the latest ASCO/CAP HR 
testing guideline.1 DCIS should be tested for ER (PR not required).
âą ER testing should be used to determine if a patient is a candidate for endocrine 
therapies. 
Cancers with 1%â100% of cells positive for ER expression are considered 
ER-positive. Patients with these results are considered eligible for endocrine 
therapies (applies to DCIS and invasive cancers). 
Invasive cancers with between 1%â10% ER positivity are considered ER-lowâ
positive. While the potential benefit of endocrine therapy is largest in patients 
with the highest percentage of ER expression, the potential for some benefit 
should also be discussed with patients with low ER expression.
Cancers with <1% staining are considered ER-negative. Patients with cancers 
with these results have not been shown to benefit from endocrine therapies. 
âą Laboratories should have standard operating procedures to maximize accuracy 
and reproducibility of results for cases with <10% ER staining or weak intensity 
staining (to avoid false negatives). The status of controls should be reported for 
cases with these results.

header:
PRINCIPLES OF BIOMARKER TESTING 
HR TESTING
content:
BINV-A
4 OF 4
1 Allison KH, Hammond MEH, Dowsett M, et al. Estrogen and progesterone receptor testing in breast cancer: ASCO/CAP guideline update. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:1346-
1366; Arch Pathol Lab Med 2020;144:545-563.
âą PR testing by IHC on invasive cancers can aid in the prognostic classification 
of cancers and serve as a control for possible false-negative ER results. 
Patients with ER-negative, PR-positive cancers may be considered for 
endocrine therapies, but the data on this group are noted to be limited. The 
same overall interpretation principles apply but PR should be interpreted as 
either positive (if 1%â100% of cells have nuclear staining) or negative (if <1% 
or 0% of cells have nuclear staining). 
âą Interpretation of any ER result by pathology should include evaluation of the 
concordance with the histologic findings of each case. Clinicians should be 
aware of when results are unusual and work with pathologists to attempt to 
resolve (eg, repeat testing, consultative review) or explain atypical reported 
findings. See tables below.

header: PRINCIPLES OF DEDICATED BREAST MRI TESTING
content:
Personnel, Facility, and Equipment
âą Breast MRI examinations are performed with IV contrast and should be performed and interpreted by an expert breast imaging team working in concert 
with the multidisciplinary treatment team. 
âą Breast MRI examinations require a dedicated breast coil and breast imaging radiologists familiar with the optimal timing sequences and other technical 
details for image interpretation. The imaging center should have the ability to perform MRI-guided needle sampling and/or image-guided localization of 
MRI-detected findings.
Clinical Indications and Applications
âą May be used for staging evaluation to define extent of cancer or presence of multifocal or multicentric cancer in the ipsilateral breast, or as screening of 
the contralateral breast cancer at time of initial diagnosis (category 2B). Available data do not demonstrate that the use of preoperative MRI improves 
re-excision rates, local recurrence, or survival for DCIS or invasive breast cancer in patients pursuing BCS.1,2
âą May be helpful for breast cancer evaluation before and after preoperative systemic therapy to define extent of disease, response to treatment, and 
potential for breast-conservation therapy.
âą May be useful in identifying otherwise clinically occult disease in patients presenting with axillary nodal metastases (cT0, cN+), with Paget disease, or 
with invasive lobular carcinoma poorly (or inadequately) defined on mammography, ultrasound, or physical examination. 
âą False-positive findings on breast MRI are common. Surgical decisions should not be based solely on the MRI findings. Additional tissue sampling of 
areas of concern identified by breast MRI is recommended.
âą The utility of MRI in follow-up screening of most patients with prior breast cancer is undefined and annual MRI is recommended in patients with a 
personal history of breast cancer who:3 
   1) were diagnosed at age â€50 or       
   2) have dense breasts
See Oncology Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis for indications for screening MRI in patients at increased breast cancer risk.

BINV-C
FERTILITY, BIRTH CONTROL, AND SEXUAL HEALTH
âą All premenopausal patients should be informed about the potential impact of chemotherapy on fertility and asked about their desire for potential future 
pregnancies. Patients who may desire future pregnancies should be referred to fertility specialists before chemotherapy and/or endocrine therapy to 
discuss the options based on patient specifics, disease stage, and biology (which determine the urgency, type, and sequence of treatment). Timing and 
duration allowed for fertility preservation, options inclusive of oocyte and embryo cryopreservation as well as evolving technologies, and the probability 
of successful pregnancies subsequent to completion of breast cancer therapy are also to be discussed.
âą Although amenorrhea frequently occurs during or after chemotherapy, it appears that the majority of patients <35 years resume menses within 2 years 
of finishing adjuvant chemotherapy.
âą Menses and fertility are not necessarily linked. Absence of regular menses, particularly if the patient is taking tamoxifen, does not necessarily imply 
infertility. Conversely, the presence of menses does not guarantee fertility. There are limited data regarding continued fertility after chemotherapy. 
âą Patients should be advised not to become pregnant while on any systemic therapy, and specific drug package inserts should be consulted for 
recommendations. 
âą A prospective trial (Partridge AH, Niman SM, Ruggeri M, et al. Breast 2021;59:327-338) evaluated premenopausal patients who had completed 
between 18â30 months of endocrine therapy before temporarily interrupting endocrine therapy for up to 2 years to allow for pregnancy. At short-term 
follow-up, the risk of recurrence was not increased among those patients who enrolled or those who became pregnant. This approach is an option for 
patients who desire pregnancy, although long-term safety is still unknown.
âą Although data are limited, hormone-based birth control is discouraged regardless of the HR status of the patient's cancer.
âą Alternative methods of birth control include non-hormonalâbased intrauterine devices (IUDs), barrier methods, or, for patients with no intent of future 
pregnancies, tubal ligation or vasectomy for the partner.
âą Randomized trials have shown that ovarian suppression with GnRH agonist therapy administered during adjuvant chemotherapy in premenopausal 
patients with breast tumors (regardless of HR status) may preserve ovarian function and diminish the likelihood of chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea.
âą Smaller historical experiences in patients with ER-positive disease have reported conflicting results with regard to the protective effect of GnRH agonist 
therapy on fertility.
âą Breastfeeding after breast-conservation therapy is not contraindicated. However, the quantity and quality of breast milk produced by the conserved 
breast may not be sufficient or may be lacking some of the nutrients needed. Breastfeeding is not recommended during active treatment with 
chemotherapy and endocrine therapy or within 6 months of completing pertuzumab or trastuzumab.
The general considerations for fertility and sexual health/function outlined for specific populations in the Oncology Guidelines for Oncology Guidelines for 
Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) Oncology and Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship are applicable to all patients diagnosed with breast cancer.

BINV-E
AXILLARY LYMPH NODE STAGING
SLNB should be performed and is the preferred method of axillary lymph node staging if the patient is an appropriate SLNB candidate  
(BINV-D).
In the absence of definitive data demonstrating superior survival, the performance of axillary staging may be considered optional in patients who have 
particularly favorable tumors, patients for whom the selection of adjuvant systemic and/or RT is unlikely to be affected, or those with serious comorbid 
conditions.a
Level III dissection to the thoracic inlet should be performed only in cases with gross disease in level II and/or lll.
In the absence of gross disease in level II nodes, lymph node dissection should include tissue inferior to the axillary vein from the latissimus dorsi (LD) 
muscle laterally to the medial border of the pectoralis minor muscle (level I/II).
Lymphedema is a potential side effect after the treatment of axillary lymph node surgery resulting from damage to the lymphatic system. Early detection/
diagnosis of lymphedema is key for optimal management. Consider pretreatment measurement of both arms as a baseline for patients with risk factors 
for lymphedema. See Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship: Lymphedema (SLYMPH-1).
a Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-negative, grade 
1â2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with 
lobular histology.

BINV-F
1 OF 2
âą Margins should be evaluated on all surgical specimens from BCS. Accurate orientation of the surgical specimens is required for optimal margin 
evaluation.
âą Pathology report should include: 
Description of the gross and microscopic margin status.
Reporting of the distance, orientation, and type of tumor (invasive or DCIS) in relation to the closest margin.
Quantification of the extent of disease that is approximating the inked margin.
âą For mammographically detected DCIS with microcalcifications, complete resection should be documented by analysis of margins and specimen 
radiography. Post-excision mammography can be considered if there is uncertainty. 
âą The Oncology Guidelines Panel accepts the definitions of negative margins after breast-conservation therapy from the 2014 SSO/ASTRO Margins Guideline1 for 
Stage I/II Invasive Cancers and the 2016 SSO/ASTRO/ASCO Guideline for DCIS.2 For patients with stage I or II invasive cancers after BCS, a positive 
margin is defined as âink on tumorâ (any invasive cancer or DCIS cells on ink). These patients generally require further surgeryâeither a re-excision 
to achieve a negative margin or a mastectomy. If re-excision is technically feasible to allow for BCS to achieve âno ink on tumor,â this can be done with 
resection of the involved margin guided by the orientation of the initial resection specimen or re-excision of the entire original excision cavity. There may 
be select patients with stage III invasive cancers who may be eligible for BCS. For these patients, the margins status would be accessed with similar 
definitions.
DCIS
âą For patients with pure DCIS treated by BCS and whole breast RT, a quantitative description of any tumor close to margin resection width of at least 2 
mm is associated with a reduced risk of ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (IBTR) relative to narrower negative margin widths, while the routine practice 
of obtaining margins >2 mm to further improve outcomes is not supported by the evidence. When there is only minimal or focal DCIS involvement near 
the margin, clinical judgment should be utilized to weigh the risks of re-excision with risk of recurrence for an individual patient.
âą For patients with DCIS treated with excision alone (no whole breast RT), regardless of margin width, there is a substantially higher rate of IBTR than 
treatment with excision and whole breast RT, even in predefined patients who are low risk. Although the optimal margin width for treatment with excision 
alone is unknown, it should be at least 2 mm, with some evidence suggesting improved IBTR rates with margin widths wider than 2 mm.
âą DCIS with microinvasion (DCIS-M), defined as an invasive focus â€1 mm in size, should refer to the DCIS margin definition when considering the optimal 
margin width (>2 mm), given that the majority of DCIS-M is comprised of DCIS and systemic therapy utilization for this lesion more closely reflects the 
treatment pattern for DCIS than for invasive carcinoma.
MARGIN STATUS RECOMMENDATIONS AFTER BREAST-CONSERVING SURGERY (BCS) FOR INVASIVE CANCERS AND DCIS
1 Moran MS, Schnitt SJ, Giuliano AE, et al. Society of Surgical Oncology-American Society for Radiation Oncology consensus guideline on margins for breast-
conserving surgery with whole-breast irradiation in stages I and II invasive breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1507-1515. 
2 Morrow M, Van Zee KJ, Solin LJ, et al. Society of Surgical Oncology-American Society for Radiation Oncology-American Society of Clinical Oncology Consensus 
Guideline on Margins for Breast-Conserving Surgery With Whole-Breast Irradiation in Ductal Carcinoma In Situ. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:4040-4046.

Invasive Breast Cancer
âą For invasive breast cancers that have a component of DCIS, regardless of the extent of DCIS, the negative margin definition of âno ink on tumorâ should 
be based on the invasive margin guideline. In this setting, âno ink on tumorâ is recommended for either DCIS or invasive cancer cells, primarily because 
the natural history, treatment, and outcomes of these lesions are more similar to invasive cancer than DCIS. For specifically challenging cases, clinical 
judgment and discussion with the patient should precede routine re-excision.
âą These margin recommendations cannot be applied directly to patients undergoing APBI/PBI,1 where data regarding local recurrence are more limited. 
Furthermore, individualized clinical judgment should be utilized on a case-by-case basis, using postoperative mammography to identify residual 
calcifications and clinical-pathologic factors such as quantitative extent of disease near margin, presence of extensive intraductal component (EIC),3 
young age, or multiple close margins to assist in identifying patients who may have an increased risk of IBTR and therefore may be selected to benefit 
from re-excision.
âą For patients with invasive breast cancer after BCS, with microscopically focally positive margins (in the absence of an EIC),3 the use of a higher 
radiation boost dose to the tumor bed may be considered, since generally a boost to the tumor bed is recommended for patients at higher risk of 
recurrence. See BINV-I.
1 Moran MS, Schnitt SJ, Giuliano AE, et al. Society of Surgical Oncology-American Society for Radiation Oncology consensus guideline on margins for breast-
conserving surgery with whole-breast irradiation in stages I and II invasive breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1507-1515. 
3 EIC is defined as an infiltrating ductal cancer where >25% of the tumor volume is DCIS and DCIS extends beyond the invasive cancer into surrounding normal breast 
parenchyma.
4 Choi J, Laws A, Hu J, et al. Margins in breast-conserving surgery after neoadjuvant therapy. Ann Surg Oncol 2018;25:3541-3547.
5 Wimmer K, Bolliger M, Bago-Horvath Z, et al. Impact of surgical margins in breast cancer after preoperative systemic chemotherapy on local recurrence and survival. 
Ann Surg Oncol 2020;27:1700-1707.

1 Boughey JC, Rosenkranz KM, Ballman KV, et al. Local recurrence after breast-conserving therapy in patients with multiple ipsilateral breast cancer: Results from 
ACOSOG Z11102 (Alliance). J Clin Oncol 2023;41:3184-3193.
2 Arthur DW, Winter KA, Kuerer HM, et al. Effectiveness of breast-conserving surgery and 3-dimensional conformal partial breast reirradiation for recurrence of breast 
cancer in the ipsilateral breast: The NRG Oncology/RTOG 1014 Phase 2 Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2020;6:75-82.
a Contraindications to radiation delivery where toxicity may be increased.
b Patients with a known genetic predisposition to breast cancer may have an increased risk of contralateral or ipsilateral breast cancers after breast-conservation 
therapy. Risk reduction strategies including prophylactic mastectomies should be discussed. See Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, 
Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate. 
c For patients >40 years of age with 2 biopsy-proven cTisâcT2 lesions (with at least one site invasive) after MRI evaluation, intending on adjuvant whole breast radiation 
with boost, breast-conservation therapy may be considered. See Boughey JC, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;41:3184-3193.
CONTRAINDICATIONS TO BREAST-CONSERVING APPROACHES REQUIRING RT
Absolute Contraindications for BCS (mastectomy is recommended)
âą Inflammatory breast cancer or invasive breast cancer with extensive skin or dermal lymphatic involvement 
âą Diffuse suspicious or malignant-appearing microcalcifications
âą Inability to clear multiple positive pathologic margins after one or more re-excision attempts, see BINV-F
âą Homozygous ATM mutation (often leads to ataxia-telangiectasia syndrome) (category 2B)a
âą Patients diagnosed with gestational breast cancer (not receiving systemic chemotherapy) who cannot receive RT within 12â16 weeksa; see PREG-1
âą Multicentric disease with any of the following criteria (mandate mastectomy)1,b:
Receipt of neoadjuvant chemotherapy or endocrine therapy
Age â€40
Triple-negative breast cancer (ER-, PR-, and HER2-negative)
More than 2 lesions involving more than 2 quadrants by MRI evaluation
Any individual lesion â„5 cm
BRCA1 and/or BRCA2 PV carrier 
Multicentric pure DCIS 
Inability to achieve negative margins (defined as no ink on tumor for invasive cancers Â± DCIS), see BINV-F
cN2âN3
Any reason for precluding the delivery of adjuvant whole breast RT + boost
Relative Contraindications for BCS (mastectomy should be considered, but breast-conserving therapy may be appropriate)
âą Patients with a known genetic predisposition to breast cancerb
âą TP53 PV carrier (eg, Li-Fraumeni syndrome) (category 2B)a,b
See Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
âą Active connective tissue disease involving the skin (eg, scleroderma or lupus)a
âą 	A history of prior RT to the affected area; knowledge of doses and volumes prescribed is importanta,c,2

a General Principles of Breast Reconstruction (BINV-H 5 of 7).
b Patient Factors Influencing Breast Reconstruction (BINV-H 6 of 7).
c An evaluation of the likely cosmetic outcome of BCS should be performed prior to surgery. 
Oncoplastic techniques for breast conservation can extend breast-conserving surgical options 
in situations where the resection by itself would likely yield an unacceptable cosmetic outcome. 
Application of these oncoplastic techniques may reduce the need for mastectomy and decrease 
the need for a secondary surgery to minimize breast deformity. Patients should be informed of 
the possibility of positive margins and potential need for secondary surgery, which could include 
either segmental re-excision, or mastectomy with or without loss of the nipple. Systematic 
oncoplastic reduction specimen orientation as well as highly specific operative documentation 
regarding tissue rearrangement should be conducted. Enhanced communication between the 
radiation oncology team and reconstructive team will be necessary for boost cavity localization 
for RT treatment planning (Shah C, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2018;25:2509-2511).
